g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we explore exec functions and IPC methods - essential concepts for understanding how operating systems manage processes and enable communication.
Exec functions replace the current program with a new one without creating a new process. The Process ID stays the same, but the entire memory image changes.
g.text: The exec family replaces the current process image with a new program.
PID remains unchanged; code, data, heap, and stack are completely replaced.
Key: fork() creates new process | exec() replaces program

g.data-narration: Inter-Process Communication (IPC) allows processes to exchange data and synchronize. Various mechanisms exist - pipes, message queues, shared memory, semaphores, and sockets.
g.text: IPC mechanisms enable processes to share data and coordinate activities.
Essential for client-server apps, parallel computing, and distributed systems.
Choose based on: speed, data volume, process relationship, direction

g.data-narration: There are six exec variants. Those with p search PATH for executables. Those with e allow environment specification. l uses list args, v uses array args.
g.text: execl, execlp, execle | execv, execvp, execve

g.data-narration: execl() takes variable arguments as a list. execlp() searches PATH. execle() additionally allows custom environment variables. All require NULL terminator.
execv() takes argument array. execvp() searches PATH with array. execve() is the underlying system call allowing environment. Most commonly used: execvp().
On success, exec never returns - control goes to new program. On failure, returns -1. PID unchanged. Close-on-exec file descriptors are closed. Signal handlers reset.
g.text: - On success: NO return (control goes to new program)

g.data-narration: Seven main IPC methods: Pipes unnamed and named, Message Queues, Shared Memory, Semaphores, Signals, Sockets, and Memory-Mapped Files. Each has unique characteristics.
Pipes are unidirectional channels. Unnamed pipes work between parent-child processes via pipe(). Named pipes (FIFOs) appear as files, allowing unrelated processes to communicate.
g.text: Appears as file | Unrelated processes can communicate

g.data-narration: Message queues are kernel-persisted message buffers with type fields for selective reading. Created with msgget(), sent via msgsnd(), received with msgrcv().
g.text: struct msgbuf { long type; char mtext[]; }

g.data-narration: Shared memory is the fastest IPC - no kernel mediation for data transfer. Use shmget() to create, shmat() to attach, shmdt() to detach. Requires synchronization via semaphores.
g.text: Shared Memory - Fastest IPC (No Kernel Mediation)
Use semaphores to prevent race conditions

g.data-narration: Semaphores are integer-valued counters for resource management. Wait (P) decrements atomically, Signal (V) increments. Binary semaphores act as mutex locks.
Signals are asynchronous notifications for event handling. Processes can catch, ignore, or use default action. SIGKILL and SIGSTOP cannot be caught or ignored.
g.text: Processes can: catch (handler), ignore, or let default action occur

g.data-narration: Sockets are the most versatile IPC - support local (Unix domain) and network (Internet domain) communication. Implement client-server model for any scale.
g.text: socket() then bind() then listen() then accept() | Client: socket() then connect()

g.data-narration: Memory-mapped files map file contents into process address space using mmap(). Enables file I/O through memory operations - useful for large files and shared memory.
g.text: ptr = mmap(NULL, len, PROT_READ|PROT_WRITE, MAP_SHARED, fd, 0);
Maps file to memory address space | File I/O through pointer operations | Also used for shared memory

g.data-narration: Here is an example using fork()+execv(). Parent forks child, child calls execv() to run ls -l. Parent waits for completion. Check exec return - it means failure!
g.text: pid = fork(); if(pid==0) { char *args[] = {"ls", "-l", NULL}; execv("/bin/ls", args); perror("exec failed"); exit(1); }
// Parent: wait(NULL); | If exec returns - error occurred!

g.data-narration: This example creates a pipe, forks. Parent writes to pipefd[1], child reads from pipefd[0]. Both must close unused ends to avoid deadlock.
g.text: pipe(pipefd); fork(); // Child: close(pipefd[1]); read(pipefd[0], buf, n); // Parent: close(pipefd[0]); write(pipefd[1], msg, len);

g.data-narration: Shared memory example: child writes to shared segment, parent reads after wait. Remember to shmdt() and use shmctl(IPC_RMID) to cleanup!
g.text: shmget(key, 4096, IPC_CREAT|0666); fork(); // Child: shmat(), strcpy(), shmdt(); // Parent: wait(), shmat(), printf(), shmdt(), shmctl(IPC_RMID);

g.data-narration: Key takeaways: exec replaces program (not create new process), variants differ in PATH/environment handling. IPC choice depends on speed, data type, and process relationship.
g.text: - exec() replaces process image, PID unchanged | 6 variants: execl/execlp/execle + execv/execvp/execve
- p = PATH search | e = custom environment | l = list args, v = array args
- exec returns ONLY on failure | fork() creates, exec() replaces
- Unnamed pipes: related processes | FIFOs: any processes
- Shared memory: fastest but needs semaphore sync

g.data-narration: Exam tips: Remember fork vs exec distinction. Shared memory is fastest. Semaphore ops are atomic. Check exec return - if returns, it failed!
Mastering exec and IPC is essential for system programming. These concepts enable building client-servers, shells, and concurrent applications. Practice with examples!
g.text: Build shells, clients-servers, and concurrent programs using these concepts
